did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based approach, specifically a pre-trained VGG-SegNet scheme, to perform automated segmentation (extraction) of Skin Melanoma (SM) lesions from Digital Dermoscopy Images (DDI). The model was trained and validated on the ISIC2016 database to extract abnormal skin lesion fragments and evaluate their performance using metrics like Jaccard Index, Dice, and Accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and validation of a pre-trained VGG-SegNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for automated segmentation of Skin Melanoma from dermoscopy images. It details a specific methodology, uses the ISIC2016 dataset for training and testing, and reports quantitative performance metrics (Jaccard Index, Dice, Accuracy) derived from new computational results, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Extraction of Abnormal Skin Lesion from Dermoscopy Image using VGG-SegNet
journal: 2021 Seventh International conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
brief_summary: This study addresses the automated segmentation of Skin Melanoma (SM) lesions from Digital Dermoscopy Images (DDI) to support early detection and reduce diagnostic burden. The authors implemented a pre-trained VGG-SegNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to extract abnormal skin lesion fragments from images with and without artifacts such as hair or medical gel. The model was trained and validated using the benchmark ISIC2016 database, which consisted of 1250 DDI images resized to 224x224x3 pixels. Performance was evaluated by comparing the segmented SM against Ground-Truth (GT) using metrics including Jaccard Index, Dice coefficient, Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, and Specificity. The proposed VGG-SegNet scheme achieved superior average performance metrics compared to existing methods, confirming its effectiveness for clinical-grade DDI assessment.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for segmentation and extraction in the study. The paper focuses on extracting the 'SM fragment' (Skin Melanoma lesion) from Digital Dermoscopy Images (DDI) using the ISIC2016 dataset. The text distinguishes skin cancer into 'non-melanoma' and 'melanoma' categories but explicitly states the work implements a scheme to extract the SM section.

Mentioned in the Introduction to categorize skin cancer types ('The skin cancer is categorized as non-melanoma and melanoma') and provide epidemiological context (3 million active non-melanoma cases globally), but not used as a target class for the segmentation experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary mask of Skin Melanoma (SM) lesion fragment
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a VGG-SegNet scheme to extract the SM section (binary form) from Digital Dermoscopy Images (DDI). The model produces a binary segmentation mask separating the lesion from the background, validated against Ground-Truth using metrics like Jaccard Index and Dice coefficient.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2016
datasets_used.description: The benchmark International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2016 database, consisting of Digital Dermoscopy Images (DDI) with various categories including clear images, images with hair, medical gel, and scale markings. Images were originally 2048x1536x3 pixels and resized to 224x224x3 pixels for the study.
classes_used: Skin Melanoma (SM) lesion fragment (binary segmentation mask)

use.training: Used to train the pre-trained VGG-SegNet scheme. The 900 training images were used along with augmented versions (horizontal/vertical flip, ±45° rotation) during the pre-training process.
use.testing: Used to test the trained system. The 350 testing images were separately tested, and the extracted binary SM fragments were compared against Ground-Truth (GT) to compute Image-Performance-Measures (IPMs).
use.other: The dataset also provided Ground-Truth (GT) masks for validation purposes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (Jaccard Index, Dice, Accuracy, etc.) and compared against existing methods in literature without statistical significance testing.

VGG-SegNet.notes: The paper reports average values of Image-Performance-Measures (IPMs) computed over all 1250 images (900 training + 350 testing) of the ISIC2016 database. The results are compared against existing methods in Table III.

ISIC2016.notes: The VGG-SegNet scheme achieved superior average performance metrics (ACC, PRE, SEN) compared to the listed existing methods. The SPE attained in earlier works (e.g., EXB, Mahnudar) were reported as better compared to VGG-SegNet. The method was tested on DDI with and without artifacts (hair, medical gel, scale markings).

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained VGG-SegNet scheme; specifically, the encoder section uses the traditional VGG16 architecture without the Fully-Connected-Layer (FCL). The model was trained on the ISIC2016 database using original and augmented images.

architecture_details.encoder_description: Down-convolution layers that convert input images into learned features. Based on VGG16 architecture without the Fully-Connected-Layer (FCL).
architecture_details.decoder_description: Up-convolution layers that convert learned features back into image dimensions.
architecture_details.output_layer: SoftMax layer for binary classification to separate Skin Melanoma (SM) from background.

training_details.hardware: Intel i5 2.5GHz processor with 16GB RAM and 2GB VRAM

classification_or_output_head.details: SoftMax classifier used to separate the SM fragment from the background, producing a binary mask.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: All Digital Dermoscopy Images (DDI) and Ground-Truth (GT) masks from the ISIC2016 database were resized from their original dimension of 2048x1536x3 pixels to 224x224x3 pixels to meet the input requirements of the VGG-SegNet scheme.
Image Augmentation: During the pre-training process, the original training images were augmented using horizontal and vertical flips, as well as rotations of ±45 degrees.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during the pre-training process of the VGG-SegNet scheme on the ISIC2016 training images.
Applied during the pre-training process of the VGG-SegNet scheme on the ISIC2016 training images.
Applied with a range of ±45 degrees during the pre-training process of the VGG-SegNet scheme on the ISIC2016 training images.

conclusions: The proposed pre-trained VGG-SegNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) scheme effectively extracts Skin Melanoma (SM) fragments from Digital Dermoscopy Images (DDI) with and without artifacts such as hair, medical gel, and scale markings.

The method achieved superior average performance metrics compared to existing literature methods, with an average Accuracy of 97.16%, Precision of 92.81%, and Sensitivity of 95.04% on the ISIC2016 database.

The VGG-SegNet scheme demonstrated robustness in handling complex DDI images containing artifacts, achieving high segmentation accuracy (e.g., 96.89% Accuracy and 99.33% Sensitivity for images with hair artifacts).
The automated segmentation approach significantly reduces the diagnostic burden and supports early detection and treatment of Skin Melanoma by providing efficient clinical-grade DDI assessment.

The study acknowledges that while the current work focuses on segmentation, future scopes include implementing VGG16-based classification to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma classes and utilizing extracted features for ABCD/ABCDE rule-supported examination.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary segmentation of Skin Melanoma (SM) lesions and does not perform classification between melanoma and non-melanoma classes, which is a critical clinical distinction.

Performance metrics (Jaccard Index, Dice, Accuracy) are averaged over all 1250 images (900 training + 350 testing) rather than strictly on the held-out test set, raising potential concerns about data leakage or optimistic bias in the reported average accuracy of 97.16%.
The paper lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the superiority of the proposed VGG-SegNet over baseline methods.
The model was trained and tested on a single dataset (ISIC2016), limiting the assessment of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
Specific hyperparameters such as the learning rate, optimizer, loss function, and number of training epochs are not reported, hindering reproducibility.

The hardware specifications (Intel i5 2.5GHz, 16GB RAM, 2GB VRAM) and software (MATLAB) used for training are modest, which may impact the scalability or speed of the solution compared to modern GPU-accelerated deep learning frameworks.

While the method handles artifacts like hair and medical gel, the specific performance degradation or robustness metrics for these artifacts are only shown for two sample images (DDI1 and DDI2) rather than aggregated statistically across the dataset.
